title: Lacto-fermentation as a food preservation method
subtitle: Salt ratios, equipment, what can go wrong, and how fermentation reduces food waste
excerpt: Lacto-fermentation uses salt to select for lactic acid bacteria, which consume sugars, produce acid, and create a stable, anaerobic environment that prevents spoilage. Sauerkraut, kimchi, and pickled vegetables made this way keep for months without refrigeration when kept in brine. This guide covers the technique, the equipment, the salt ratios, and the common failures.
sourceNotes: USDA Complete Guide to Home Canning on fermented vegetables. Sandor Katz, The Art of Fermentation (Chelsea Green Publishing, public domain excerpt). UK Food Standards Agency guidance on home fermentation food safety. Nourished Kitchen guidance on lacto-fermentation ratios.

glossaryTerms.definition: A preservation method in which Lactobacillus bacteria convert sugars to lactic acid in an anaerobic (oxygen-free) environment. The acid pH prevents pathogenic bacteria. Salt concentration selects for Lactobacillus over less tolerant organisms.

The percentage of salt by weight in the water or total vegetable mass. A 2 percent brine by weight (20 g salt per 1 kg of vegetables or 20 g salt per 1 litre of water) is the standard starting point for lacto-fermented vegetables.

content.text: Lacto-fermentation extends the life of vegetables by months and in some cases years without refrigeration or specialist equipment. A head of cabbage that would spoil in two to three weeks raw keeps six to twelve months as sauerkraut. A glut of cucumbers, green beans, or cauliflower can be preserved the same way.

Salt at the right concentration creates conditions that Lactobacillus tolerates and putrefactive bacteria do not. The bacteria are present on the raw vegetable surface. They need no starter culture: the wild fermentation begins within 24 to 48 hours. The
produces lactic acid, which drops the pH to 3.5 to 4.5. At this pH, virtually no harmful pathogens survive.

is 2 percent by weight. For sauerkraut (dry salt method): weigh the shredded cabbage, calculate 2 percent of its weight, add that much non-iodised salt, and massage until brine is released. Pack tightly into a jar so the brine covers the cabbage. For whole vegetables (wet brine method): dissolve 20 g of non-iodised salt per litre of water and submerge the vegetables. A 2 percent brine produces a mild, well-balanced result. 3 percent gives a saltier, slower ferment that keeps longer. Below 1.5 percent, the risk of putrefaction increases.
A wide-mouth Kilner jar or ceramic crock. The container does not need to be airtight; gas produced by fermentation must be able to escape.
A weight to keep vegetables submerged below the brine line. A small jar filled with brine placed inside the jar works; a ziplock bag filled with brine pressed on top works.
Non-iodised salt (iodine inhibits Lactobacillus growth). Sea salt, kosher salt, and pickling salt are all suitable.

Fermentation is active at room temperature (18 to 22 degrees Celsius) for 5 to 14 days. The jar should be placed on a tray (it may bubble over). Taste after 5 days: if it is pleasantly sour, move it to the fridge or a cool larder. In a fridge at 4 degrees Celsius, fermentation slows to near-zero and the product keeps for six months. In a cool cellar at 10 degrees Celsius, it continues to ferment slowly and peaks in flavour at 4 to 6 weeks.
This is kahm yeast, a harmless wild yeast. Skim it off and push the vegetables back below the brine. It does not indicate spoilage. A stronger brine (2.5 percent) reduces kahm.

attrs.symptom: Pink, brown, or black discolouration, or foul smell

content.text: Discard the batch. True spoilage is visually and olfactorily distinct from normal fermentation. Lacto-fermented vegetables smell sour and funky; spoiled vegetables smell putrid. When in doubt, discard.
